question:
Problem A-3

Let \( p \) be in the set \( \{3,5,7,11, \ldots\} \) of odd primes and let
\[
F(n)=1+2 n+3 n^{2}+\cdots+(p-1) n^{p-2} .
\]

Prove that if \( a \) and \( b \) are distinct integers in \( \{0,1,2, \ldots, p-1\} \) then \( F(a) \) and \( F(b) \) are not congruent modulo \( p \), that is, \( F(a)-F(b) \) is not exactly divisible by \( p \).
solution:
A-3.
\[
\begin{aligned}
F(n) & =1+2 n+3 n^{2}+\cdots+(p-1) n^{p-2}, \\
n F(n) & =n+2 n^{2}+\cdots+(p-2) n^{p-2}+(p-1) n^{p-1} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence \( (1-n) F(n)=\left(1+n+n^{2}+\cdots+n^{p-2}\right)-(p-1) n^{p-1} \) and similarly
\[
(1-n)^{2} F(n)=1-n^{p-1}-(1-n)(p-1) n^{p-1}=1-p \cdot n^{p-1}+(p-1) n^{p} .
\]

Modulo \( p, n^{p} \equiv n \) by the Little Fermat Theorem and so \( (1-n)^{2} F(n) \equiv 1-n \). If neither \( a \) nor \( b \) is congruent to \( 1(\bmod p), 1-a \neq 1-b \) and there are distinct reciprocals \( (1-a)^{-1} \) and \( (1-b)^{-1}(\bmod p) \); then
\[
f(a) \equiv(1-a)^{-1}, f(b) \equiv(1-b)^{-1}, f(a) \equiv f(b)(\bmod p) .
\]

If one of \( a \) and \( b \), say \( a \), is congruent to 1 , then \( b \neq 0(\bmod p) \) and so \( f(b) \equiv(1-b)^{-1} \neq 0 \) \( (\bmod p) \) while
\[
f(a)=1+2+\cdots+(p-1)=p(p-1) / 2 \equiv 0(\bmod p) .
\]

descriptive_long.question:
Problem A-3

Let \( primeodd \) be in the set \( \{3,5,7,11, \ldots\} \) of odd primes and let
\[
funcsum(integern)=1+2 integern+3 integern^{2}+\cdots+(primeodd-1) integern^{primeodd-2} .
\]

Prove that if \( varalpha \) and \( varbravo \) are distinct integers in \{0,1,2, \ldots, primeodd-1\} then \( funcsum(varalpha) \) and \( funcsum(varbravo) \) are not congruent modulo \( primeodd \), that is, \( funcsum(varalpha)-funcsum(varbravo) \) is not exactly divisible by \( primeodd \).
descriptive_long.solution:
A-3.
\[
\begin{aligned}
funcsum(integern) & =1+2 integern+3 integern^{2}+\cdots+(primeodd-1) integern^{primeodd-2}, \\
integern funcsum(integern) & =integern+2 integern^{2}+\cdots+(primeodd-2) integern^{primeodd-2}+(primeodd-1) integern^{primeodd-1} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence \( (1-integern) funcsum(integern)=\left(1+integern+integern^{2}+\cdots+integern^{primeodd-2}\right)-(primeodd-1) integern^{primeodd-1} \) and similarly
\[
(1-integern)^{2} funcsum(integern)=1-integern^{primeodd-1}-(1-integern)(primeodd-1) integern^{primeodd-1}=1-primeodd \cdot integern^{primeodd-1}+(primeodd-1) integern^{primeodd} .
\]

Modulo \( primeodd, integern^{primeodd} \equiv integern \) by the Little Fermat Theorem and so \( (1-integern)^{2} funcsum(integern) \equiv 1-integern \). If neither \( varalpha \) nor \( varbravo \) is congruent to \( 1(\bmod primeodd), 1-varalpha \neq 1-varbravo \) and there are distinct reciprocals \( (1-varalpha)^{-1} \) and \( (1-varbravo)^{-1}(\bmod primeodd) \); then
\[
funclow(varalpha) \equiv(1-varalpha)^{-1}, funclow(varbravo) \equiv(1-varbravo)^{-1}, funclow(varalpha) \equiv funclow(varbravo)(\bmod primeodd) .
\]

If one of \( varalpha \) and \( varbravo \), say \( varalpha \), is congruent to 1 , then \( varbravo \neq 0(\bmod primeodd) \) and so \( funclow(varbravo) \equiv(1-varbravo)^{-1} \neq 0 \) \( (\bmod primeodd) \) while
\[
funclow(varalpha)=1+2+\cdots+(primeodd-1)=primeodd(primeodd-1) / 2 \equiv 0(\bmod primeodd) .
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem A-3

Let \( headlight \) be in the set \( \{3,5,7,11, \ldots\} \) of odd primes and let
\[
sandcastle(grapefruit)=1+2 grapefruit+3 grapefruit^{2}+\cdots+(headlight-1) grapefruit^{headlight-2} .
\]

Prove that if \( shoelaces \) and \( firecracker \) are distinct integers in \( \{0,1,2, \ldots, headlight-1\} \) then \( sandcastle(shoelaces) \) and \( sandcastle(firecracker) \) are not congruent modulo \( headlight \), that is, \( sandcastle(shoelaces)-sandcastle(firecracker) \) is not exactly divisible by \( headlight \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-3.
\[
\begin{aligned}
sandcastle(grapefruit) & =1+2 grapefruit+3 grapefruit^{2}+\cdots+(headlight-1) grapefruit^{headlight-2}, \\
grapefruit\, sandcastle(grapefruit) & =grapefruit+2 grapefruit^{2}+\cdots+(headlight-2) grapefruit^{headlight-2}+(headlight-1) grapefruit^{headlight-1} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence \( (1-grapefruit) sandcastle(grapefruit)=\left(1+grapefruit+grapefruit^{2}+\cdots+grapefruit^{headlight-2}\right)-(headlight-1) grapefruit^{headlight-1} \) and similarly
\[
(1-grapefruit)^{2} sandcastle(grapefruit)=1-grapefruit^{headlight-1}-(1-grapefruit)(headlight-1) grapefruit^{headlight-1}=1-headlight \cdot grapefruit^{headlight-1}+(headlight-1) grapefruit^{headlight} .
\]

Modulo \( headlight, grapefruit^{headlight} \equiv grapefruit \) by the Little Fermat Theorem and so \( (1-grapefruit)^{2} sandcastle(grapefruit) \equiv 1-grapefruit \). If neither \( shoelaces \) nor \( firecracker \) is congruent to \( 1(\bmod headlight)\), \( 1-shoelaces \neq 1-firecracker \) and there are distinct reciprocals \( (1-shoelaces)^{-1} \) and \( (1-firecracker)^{-1}(\bmod headlight) \); then
\[
tortoise(shoelaces) \equiv(1-shoelaces)^{-1}, \quad tortoise(firecracker) \equiv(1-firecracker)^{-1}, \quad tortoise(shoelaces) \equiv tortoise(firecracker)(\bmod headlight) .
\]

If one of \( shoelaces \) and \( firecracker \), say \( shoelaces \), is congruent to 1, then \( firecracker \neq 0(\bmod headlight) \) and so \( tortoise(firecracker) \equiv(1-firecracker)^{-1} \neq 0 \) \( (\bmod headlight) \) while
\[
tortoise(shoelaces)=1+2+\cdots+(headlight-1)=headlight(headlight-1) / 2 \equiv 0(\bmod headlight) .
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem A-3

Let \( compositenum \) be in the set \( \{3,5,7,11, \ldots\} \) of odd primes and let
\[
staticfunc(constantval)=1+2 constantval+3 constantval^{2}+\cdots+(compositenum-1) constantval^{compositenum-2} .
\]

Prove that if \( finalvalue \) and \( knownvalue \) are distinct integers in \( \{0,1,2, \ldots, compositenum-1\} \) then \( staticfunc(finalvalue) \) and \( staticfunc(knownvalue) \) are not congruent modulo \( compositenum \), that is, \( staticfunc(finalvalue)-staticfunc(knownvalue) \) is not exactly divisible by \( compositenum \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-3.
\[
\begin{aligned}
staticfunc(constantval) & =1+2 constantval+3 constantval^{2}+\cdots+(compositenum-1) constantval^{compositenum-2}, \\
constantval staticfunc(constantval) & =constantval+2 constantval^{2}+\cdots+(compositenum-2) constantval^{compositenum-2}+(compositenum-1) constantval^{compositenum-1} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence \( (1-constantval) staticfunc(constantval)=\left(1+constantval+constantval^{2}+\cdots+constantval^{compositenum-2}\right)-(compositenum-1) constantval^{compositenum-1} \) and similarly
\[
(1-constantval)^{2} staticfunc(constantval)=1-constantval^{compositenum-1}-(1-constantval)(compositenum-1) constantval^{compositenum-1}=1-compositenum \cdot constantval^{compositenum-1}+(compositenum-1) constantval^{compositenum} .
\]

Modulo \( compositenum, constantval^{compositenum} \equiv constantval \) by the Little Fermat Theorem and so \( (1-constantval)^{2} staticfunc(constantval) \equiv 1-constantval \). If neither \( finalvalue \) nor \( knownvalue \) is congruent to \( 1(\bmod compositenum), 1-finalvalue \neq 1-knownvalue \) and there are distinct reciprocals \( (1-finalvalue)^{-1} \) and \( (1-knownvalue)^{-1}(\bmod compositenum) \); then
\[
dynamicconst(finalvalue) \equiv(1-finalvalue)^{-1}, dynamicconst(knownvalue) \equiv(1-knownvalue)^{-1}, dynamicconst(finalvalue) \equiv dynamicconst(knownvalue)(\bmod compositenum) .
\]

If one of \( finalvalue \) and \( knownvalue \), say \( finalvalue \), is congruent to 1 , then \( knownvalue \neq 0(\bmod compositenum) \) and so \( dynamicconst(knownvalue) \equiv(1-knownvalue)^{-1} \neq 0 \) \( (\bmod compositenum) \) while
\[
dynamicconst(finalvalue)=1+2+\cdots+(compositenum-1)=compositenum(compositenum-1) / 2 \equiv 0(\bmod compositenum) .
\]

garbled_string.question:
Problem A-3

Let \( gfzlonpai \) be in the set \( \{3,5,7,11, \ldots\} \) of odd primes and let
\[
rqtsvelon(zqkmrptu)=1+2 zqkmrptu+3 zqkmrptu^{2}+\cdots+(gfzlonpai-1) zqkmrptu^{gfzlonpai-2} .
\]

Prove that if \( hvnqslta \) and \( cxdmqfye \) are distinct integers in \( \{0,1,2, \ldots, gfzlonpai-1\} \) then \( rqtsvelon(hvnqslta) \) and \( rqtsvelon(cxdmqfye) \) are not congruent modulo \( gfzlonpai \), that is, \( rqtsvelon(hvnqslta)-rqtsvelon(cxdmqfye) \) is not exactly divisible by \( gfzlonpai \).
garbled_string.solution:
A-3.
\[
\begin{aligned}
rqtsvelon(zqkmrptu) & =1+2 zqkmrptu+3 zqkmrptu^{2}+\cdots+(gfzlonpai-1) zqkmrptu^{gfzlonpai-2}, \\
zqkmrptu \, rqtsvelon(zqkmrptu) & =zqkmrptu+2 zqkmrptu^{2}+\cdots+(gfzlonpai-2) zqkmrptu^{gfzlonpai-2}+(gfzlonpai-1) zqkmrptu^{gfzlonpai-1} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence \( (1-zqkmrptu) rqtsvelon(zqkmrptu)=\left(1+zqkmrptu+zqkmrptu^{2}+\cdots+zqkmrptu^{gfzlonpai-2}\right)-(gfzlonpai-1) zqkmrptu^{gfzlonpai-1} \) and similarly
\[
(1-zqkmrptu)^{2} rqtsvelon(zqkmrptu)=1-zqkmrptu^{gfzlonpai-1}-(1-zqkmrptu)(gfzlonpai-1) zqkmrptu^{gfzlonpai-1}=1-gfzlonpai \cdot zqkmrptu^{gfzlonpai-1}+(gfzlonpai-1) zqkmrptu^{gfzlonpai} .
\]

Modulo \( gfzlonpai, zqkmrptu^{gfzlonpai} \equiv zqkmrptu \) by the Little Fermat Theorem and so \( (1-zqkmrptu)^{2} rqtsvelon(zqkmrptu) \equiv 1-zqkmrptu \). If neither \( hvnqslta \) nor \( cxdmqfye \) is congruent to \( 1(\bmod gfzlonpai), 1-hvnqslta \neq 1-cxdmqfye \) and there are distinct reciprocals \( (1-hvnqslta)^{-1} \) and \( (1-cxdmqfye)^{-1}(\bmod gfzlonpai) \); then
\[
mpxravilo(hvnqslta) \equiv(1-hvnqslta)^{-1}, \quad mpxravilo(cxdmqfye) \equiv(1-cxdmqfye)^{-1}, \quad mpxravilo(hvnqslta) \equiv mpxravilo(cxdmqfye)(\bmod gfzlonpai) .
\]

If one of \( hvnqslta \) and \( cxdmqfye \), say \( hvnqslta \), is congruent to 1 , then \( cxdmqfye \neq 0(\bmod gfzlonpai) \) and so \( mpxravilo(cxdmqfye) \equiv(1-cxdmqfye)^{-1} \neq 0 \, (\bmod gfzlonpai) \) while
\[
mpxravilo(hvnqslta)=1+2+\cdots+(gfzlonpai-1)=gfzlonpai(gfzlonpai-1) / 2 \equiv 0(\bmod gfzlonpai) .
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let $p$ be an odd prime and denote  
\[
v_{p}(\,\cdot\,)\quad (v_{p}(p)=1),\qquad 
|\cdot|_{p}=p^{-v_{p}(\,\cdot\,)}
\]
for the $p$-adic valuation on $\mathbf Z_{p}$ and the associated absolute value.  
Put  
\[
F(X)=1+2X+3X^{2}+\dots +(p-1)X^{p-2}\in\mathbf Z[X].\tag{1}
\]

For every integer $k\ge 1$ consider the reduction  
\[
\varphi_{k}\;:\;\mathbf Z/p^{k}\mathbf Z\longrightarrow \mathbf Z/p^{k}\mathbf Z,\qquad 
x\longmapsto F(x)\pmod{p^{k}}.\tag{2}
\]

For $k\ge 2$ define the {\em highly ramified} set  
\[
A_{k}:=\bigl\{\,1+p^{\,k-1}u : u\in\mathbf Z/p\mathbf Z\bigr\}\subset\mathbf Z/p^{k}\mathbf Z.\tag{3}
\]

Inside the $p$-adic unit disc  
\[
D=\bigl\{x\in\mathbf Z_{p}:|x|_{p}\le 1\bigr\}
\]
put  
\[
\Omega=\bigl\{x\in\mathbf Z_{p}:|x|_{p}=1\;\text{ and }\;x\not\equiv 1\pmod p\bigr\}.\tag{4}
\]

Answer the following questions.

(a) Behaviour modulo high powers of $p$.

(i) Show that $\varphi_{1}$ is a permutation of the finite field $\mathbf F_{p}$.

(ii) For $k\ge 2$ prove  

\[
\boxed{p\ge 5}\;:\;
\varphi_{k}\text{ is {\em constant} on }A_{k}\text{ and the common value equals }F(1)\pmod{p^{k}}.
\]

\[
\boxed{p=3}\;:\;
\varphi_{k}\bigl(A_{k}\bigr)=
\bigl\{\,F(1)+ 2\cdot 3^{\,k-1}u : u\in\mathbf Z/3\mathbf Z\bigr\}\subset 3\mathbf Z/3^{k}\mathbf Z,
\]
and $\varphi_{k}|_{A_{k}}$ is injective.  
In particular $\varphi_{k}(A_{k})\cap A_{k}=\varnothing$ for every $k\ge 2$.

(iii) Describe the fibres of $\varphi_{k}$.

 * Case $p\ge 5$.

  Let $k\ge 2$ and write $t:=v_{p}(x-1)$ for $x\in\mathbf Z/p^{k}\mathbf Z$ ($0\le t\le k$).

  (\alpha )  If $t=0$ (that is, $x\not\equiv 1\pmod p$) show that  
\[
\bigl|\varphi_{k}^{-1}\!\bigl(\varphi_{k}(x)\bigr)\bigr|=1 .
\]

  (\beta )  If $1\le t\le k-1$ write $x=1+p^{t}u$ with $u\in\mathbf Z/p^{\,k-t}\mathbf Z$ and $p\nmid u$.  
Prove that  

\[
\varphi_{k}\!\bigl(1+p^{t}u_{1}\bigr)\equiv
\varphi_{k}\!\bigl(1+p^{t}u_{2}\bigr)\pmod{p^{k}}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 
u_{1}\equiv u_{2}\pmod{p^{\,k-t-1}}.
\]

  Deduce that {\em every} non-trivial fibre of $\varphi_{k}$ has cardinality $p$, that it is contained in the set  
\[
C_{k,t}:=\bigl\{\,1+p^{t}\bigl(u_{0}+p^{\,k-t-1}\ell\bigr):\ell\in\mathbf Z/p\mathbf Z\bigr\},
\]
and that, for each fixed $t$, the images of the different fibres are pairwise distinct.  In particular  

\[
\#\,\operatorname{im}(\varphi_{k})\;=\;(p-1)p^{\,k-1}+\frac{p^{\,k-1}-1}{p-1},
\]
so exactly 
\[
\boxed{\displaystyle\frac{(p-2)p^{\,k-1}+1}{p-1}}
\]
residue classes are omitted.

 * Case $p=3$.

  Prove that $\varphi_{k}$ is bijective for all $k\ge 1$ and that every fibre has cardinality $1$.

(b) A universal $p$-adic inverse away from the ramified fibre.

Construct a map  
\[
P\;:\;\Omega\longrightarrow\Omega\tag{5}
\]
such that $F\circ P=P\circ F=\mathrm{id}$ on $\Omega$.  
Prove that $P$ is $1$-Lipschitz (hence uniformly continuous) and locally analytic on $\Omega$.

(c) Global analytic structure.

Show that $P$ is the restriction to $\Omega$ of a power series  
\[
P(X)=\sum_{j\ge 0}c_{j}X^{\,j},\qquad c_{j}\in\mathbf Z_{p},\qquad |c_{j}|_{p}\longrightarrow 0,\tag{6}
\]
whose radius of convergence is $\ge 1$.  
Deduce that $F$ restricts to a $p$-adic analytic diffeomorphism of $\Omega$ with inverse $P$.

All arguments have to be carried out purely in the $p$-adic setting; no appeal to complex analysis or formal-group theory is allowed.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we keep the notation of the problem; all unnamed congruences are taken in $\mathbf Z$.

0.  An algebraic identity.  
Put  
\[
S(X)=1+X+\dots+X^{p-1}=\frac{1-X^{p}}{1-X},\qquad  F(X)=S'(X).
\]
Differentiating $(1-X)S(X)=1-X^{p}$ gives  
\[
(1-X)^{2}F(X)=1-pX^{p-1}+(p-1)X^{p}.\tag{$\ast$}
\]

1.  Proof of (a)(i).  
Fix $x\in\mathbf F_{p}$.  Reducing $(\ast)$ modulo $p$ and evaluating at $x$ yields  
\[
(1-x)^{2}F(x)\equiv1-x^{p}\equiv1-x\pmod p ,
\]
where the second equivalence uses Fermat's little theorem.  
If $x\not\equiv1\pmod p$ we may cancel $(1-x)$ and obtain  
\[
F(x)\equiv(1-x)^{-1}\pmod p.\tag{1.1}
\]
Thus $F$ acts on $\mathbf F_{p}\setminus\{1\}$ by $x\mapsto(1-x)^{-1}$, hence bijectively.  
Because  
\[
F(1)=\sum_{j=1}^{p-1}j=\frac{p(p-1)}{2}\equiv0\pmod p ,
\]
the value $0$ is taken exactly at $x\equiv1\pmod p$.  
Consequently $\varphi_{1}$ is a permutation of $\mathbf F_{p}$.  \hfill$\square$

2.  Local expansion around $X=1$.  
Write $X=1+Z$ and put $G(X)=(1-X)^{2}F(X)$.  By $(\ast)$,
\[
G(1+Z)=1-p(1+Z)^{p-1}+(p-1)(1+Z)^{p}.
\]
A Taylor expansion at $Z=0$ gives  
\[
G(1+Z)=\frac{p(p-1)}{2}Z^{2}+\frac{p(p-1)(p-2)}{3}Z^{3}+Z^{4}H(Z),\tag{2.1}
\]
with $H(Z)\in\mathbf Z_{p}[[Z]]$.  Because $F(1+Z)=G(1+Z)/Z^{2}$ we have  
\[
F(1+Z)=F(1)+Z\,R(Z),\qquad  
R(Z)=\frac{p(p-1)(p-2)}{3}+Z\,H(Z)\in\mathbf Z_{p}[[Z]].\tag{2.2}
\]
Observe  
\[
v_{p}\bigl(R(0)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
1,& p\ge 5,\\
0,& p=3.
\end{cases}\tag{2.3}
\]

Lemma 2.1. Let $t\ge 1$ and $u\in\mathbf Z$ with $p\nmid u$, and set $a=1+p^{t}u$. Then  
\[
v_{p}\bigl(F(a)-F(1)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
t+1,& p\ge 5,\\
t,& p=3.
\end{cases}
\]

Proof. Insert $Z=p^{t}u$ into (2.2) and use (2.3).  \hfill$\square$

We shall also need a valuation estimate for {\em differences}.

Lemma 2.2. Let $p\ge 5$, $k\ge 2$, $1\le t\le k-1$ and $u_{1},u_{2}\in\mathbf Z$ with $p\nmid u_{1}u_{2}$.  
Put $x_{i}=1+p^{t}u_{i}$ ($i=1,2$).  Then  
\[
v_{p}\bigl(F(x_{1})-F(x_{2})\bigr)=
\min\bigl\{t+1+v_{p}(u_{1}-u_{2}),\,2t\bigr\}.\tag{2.4}
\]

Proof.  
Write $Z_{i}=p^{t}u_{i}$ and expand using (2.2):
\[
F(1+Z_{1})-F(1+Z_{2})=(Z_{1}-Z_{2})\,R(0)+(Z_{1}-Z_{2})\,Z_{2}H(Z_{2})+Z_{1}(Z_{1}-Z_{2})H(Z_{1}).
\]
The first term has valuation $t+1+v_{p}(u_{1}-u_{2})$, the two others at least $2t$.  
Taking the minimum gives (2.4).  \hfill$\square$

(The case $k=2$ is included: for $k=2$ we have $t=1$, so $2t=2>k=2$ is false, hence the minimum in (2.4) is the first entry as required for the later application.)

3.  Proof of (a)(ii).

3.1  Case $p\ge 5$.  
Take $x\in A_{k}$, so $x=1+p^{\,k-1}u$ with $u\bmod p$.  
Lemma 2.1 with $t=k-1$ gives $v_{p}\bigl(F(x)-F(1)\bigr)=k$, hence  
\[
F(x)\equiv F(1)\pmod{p^{k}}.
\]
Thus $\varphi_{k}$ is constant on $A_{k}$ with the stated value.

3.2  Case $p=3$.  
Let $x=1+3^{\,k-1}u\in A_{k}$.  
Lemma 2.1 yields
\[
F(x)-F(1)=3^{\,k-1}u\,R\bigl(3^{\,k-1}u\bigr)\equiv 2\cdot3^{\,k-1}u\pmod{3^{k}},
\]
because $R(0)=\frac{3(3-1)(3-2)}{3}=2$ is a unit.  
Therefore
\[
\varphi_{k}(x)\equiv F(1)+2\cdot3^{\,k-1}u\pmod{3^{k}},
\]
which is the claimed description of the image.  
Different $u$'s give different residues modulo $3^{k}$, so $\varphi_{k}|_{A_{k}}$ is injective.  
In particular $\varphi_{k}(A_{k})\cap A_{k}=\varnothing$. \hfill$\square$

4.  Fibres for $p\ge 5$ - proof of (a)(iii).

4.1  Injectivity outside the residue $1\pmod p$ (statement (\alpha )).  
Let $x,y\in\mathbf Z/p^{k}\mathbf Z$ with $x\not\equiv1\pmod p\neq y$ and assume  
\[
F(x)\equiv F(y)\pmod{p^{k}}.\tag{4.1}
\]
Because $x\not\equiv1\pmod p$, (1.1) gives $F'(x)\equiv(1-x)^{-2}\pmod p$, hence $F'(x)$ is a $p$-adic unit.  
A first-order Taylor expansion with integral remainder shows
\[
v_{p}\bigl(F(y)-F(x)\bigr)=v_{p}(y-x).\tag{4.2}
\]
Condition (4.1) forces $v_{p}(y-x)\ge k$, hence $x\equiv y\pmod{p^{k}}$.  
Thus $\varphi_{k}$ is injective on $\{x\not\equiv1\pmod p\}$ and (\alpha ) is proved.

4.2  Elements congruent to $1\pmod p$ (statement (\beta )).  

Fix $k\ge 2$ and $1\le t\le k-1$.  Write
\[
x_{i}=1+p^{t}u_{i},\quad u_{i}\in\mathbf Z/p^{\,k-t}\mathbf Z,\;p\nmid u_{i}\;(i=1,2).
\]
By Lemma 2.2
\[
v_{p}\bigl(F(x_{1})-F(x_{2})\bigr)\ge k
\Longleftrightarrow
t+1+v_{p}(u_{1}-u_{2})\ge k,
\]
because $2t\le k-1+k-1<k$ if $t\le k-2$, whereas $2t=k+1>k$ for $t=k-1$.  Hence
\[
\varphi_{k}(x_{1})\equiv\varphi_{k}(x_{2})\pmod{p^{k}}
\Longleftrightarrow
u_{1}\equiv u_{2}\pmod{p^{\,k-t-1}}.\tag{4.3}
\]

(i)  {\em Constant fibre for $t=k-1$.}  
Here $k-t-1=0$, so (4.3) is automatically satisfied; as there are $p$ different $u$'s, $A_{k}=C_{k,k-1}$ is the unique fibre over $F(1)$ and has size $p$.

(ii)  {\em Uniform fibres of size $p$ for $1\le t\le k-2$.}  
The set $C_{k,t}$ contains exactly $p$ distinct values of $u$, hence (4.3) shows that $|\,\varphi_{k}^{-1}(y)|=p$ for every $y$ in the image of $C_{k,t}$.  
Because $u\mapsto u\bmod p^{\,k-t-1}$ has $p^{\,k-t-1}$ distinct values, the images of $C_{k,t}$ consist of $p^{\,k-t-1}$ pairwise different classes.

(iii)  {\em Counting the image.}  
Injectivity on the complement of $1+p\mathbf Z/p^{k}\mathbf Z$ gives $(p-1)p^{\,k-1}$ image points.  
Adding the contributions from $t=1,\dots ,k-1$ we obtain
\[
\sum_{t=1}^{k-1}p^{\,k-t-1}=p^{\,k-2}+p^{\,k-3}+\dots+1=\frac{p^{\,k-1}-1}{p-1}.
\]
Hence
\[
\#\,\operatorname{im}(\varphi_{k})
=(p-1)p^{\,k-1}+\frac{p^{\,k-1}-1}{p-1},
\]
so the number of omitted residue classes equals
\[
p^{\,k}-\#\,\operatorname{im}(\varphi_{k})
=\frac{(p-2)p^{\,k-1}+1}{p-1},
\]
in agreement with the corrected statement.  \hfill$\square$

5.  Proof of (a)(iii) for $p=3$.  
Here $F(X)=1+2X$ and slope $2$ is a unit in every $\mathbf Z/3^{k}\mathbf Z$.  
Hence $\varphi_{k}(x)=1+2x$ is bijective on $\mathbf Z/3^{k}\mathbf Z$, so every fibre has size $1$.  \hfill$\square$

6.  Preparation for part (b).

Lemma 6.1. $\Omega$ is stable under $F$.

Proof. If $x\in\Omega$, then $|x|_{p}=1$ and $x\not\equiv1\pmod p$.  
By (1.1) $F(x)\equiv(1-x)^{-1}\not\equiv0,1\pmod p$, hence $|F(x)|_{p}=1$ and $F(x)\not\equiv1\pmod p$.  \hfill$\square$

7.  Construction of the inverse - proof of (b).

For $y\in\Omega$ choose the unique residue  
\[
x_{0}\equiv1-y^{-1}\pmod p,\qquad x_{0}\not\equiv1\pmod p.
\]
Because $F'(x_{0})\equiv(1-x_{0})^{-2}\not\equiv0\pmod p$, Hensel's lemma provides a {\em unique} lift $x\in\mathbf Z_{p}$ with $x\equiv x_{0}\pmod p$ and $F(x)=y$.  
By Lemma 6.1, $x\in\Omega$ as well.  Define  
\[
P:\Omega\longrightarrow\Omega,\qquad P(y)=x.\tag{7.1}
\]
Then $F\circ P=P\circ F=\mathrm{id}_{\Omega}$ by construction.

7.1  Isometry property.  
Take $y,y'\in\Omega$ and set $x=P(y),\;x'=P(y')$.  
Using a first-order Taylor formula one shows exactly as in 4.1 that  
\[
|y-y'|_{p}=|x'-x|_{p}.
\]
So $P$ is an isometry, in particular $1$-Lipschitz and uniformly continuous on $\Omega$.

7.2  Local analyticity.  
Because $F'(u)$ is a unit on $\Omega$, the $p$-adic implicit-function theorem yields analyticity of $P$ in a neighbourhood of every point of $\Omega$.

8.  Global power-series representation - proof of (c).

8.1  A globally $1$-Lipschitz extension.  
Define  
\[
\widetilde P(x)\;:=\;
\begin{cases}
P(x), & x\in\Omega,\\[4pt]
1, & x\notin\Omega
\end{cases}\qquad\bigl(x\in D\bigr).\tag{8.1}
\]
To see that $\widetilde P$ is $1$-Lipschitz on $D$ there are three cases.

(i) $x,y\in\Omega$: already done in 7.1.  

(ii) $x,y\notin\Omega$: $\widetilde P(x)=\widetilde P(y)=1$, so the inequality is trivial.  

(iii) $x\in\Omega$, $y\notin\Omega$: then $\widetilde P(y)=1$ and $|x-1|_{p}=|x|_{p}=1$, whereas $|x-y|_{p}\le1$ because both $x,y\in D$.  Hence
\[
|\widetilde P(x)-\widetilde P(y)|_{p}=|x-1|_{p}=1\le |x-y|_{p}.
\]

8.2  Mahler expansion.  
Mahler's theorem now gives a convergent expansion  
\[
\widetilde P(X)=\sum_{j\ge 0}a_{j}\binom{X}{j},\qquad 
a_{j}\in\mathbf Z_{p},\quad a_{j}\xrightarrow{j\to\infty}0.\tag{8.2}
\]

8.3  Passage to the monomial basis.  
Standard estimates (Amice-Velu, Legendre) yield coefficients $c_{m}\in\mathbf Z_{p}$ with $|c_{m}|_{p}\to0$ such that  
\[
\widetilde P(X)=\sum_{m\ge 0}c_{m}X^{\,m},\qquad 
\text{radius of convergence }\ge 1.\tag{8.3}
\]
Restricting (8.3) to $\Omega$ gives the series (6), proving part (c).

8.4 Conclusion.  
Because both $F$ and $P$ are analytic on $\Omega$ and satisfy $F\circ P=P\circ F=\mathrm{id}_{\Omega}$, $F$ is a $p$-adic analytic diffeomorphism of $\Omega$ with inverse $P$.  \hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $p$ be an odd prime and denote  
\[
v_{p}(\,\cdot\,)\;(v_{p}(p)=1),\qquad 
|\cdot|_{p}=p^{-v_{p}(\,\cdot\,)}
\]
for the $p$-adic valuation on $\mathbf Q_{p}$ and its absolute value.  
Put  
\[
F(X)=1+2X+3X^{2}+\dots +(p-1)X^{p-2}\in\mathbf Z[X].\tag{1}
\]

For every integer $k\ge 1$ consider the reduction  
\[
\varphi_{k}\;:\;\mathbf Z/p^{k}\mathbf Z\longrightarrow \mathbf Z/p^{k}\mathbf Z,\qquad 
x\longmapsto F(x)\pmod{p^{k}}.\tag{2}
\]

For $k\ge 2$ define the {\em highly ramified} set  
\[
A_{k}:=\bigl\{\,1+p^{\,k-1}u : u\in\mathbf Z/p\mathbf Z\bigr\}\subset\mathbf Z/p^{k}\mathbf Z.\tag{3}
\]

Inside the $p$-adic unit disc  
\[
D=\bigl\{x\in\mathbf Z_{p}:|x|_{p}\le 1\bigr\}
\]
put  
\[
\Omega=\bigl\{x\in\mathbf Z_{p}:|x|_{p}=1\;\text{ and }\;x\not\equiv 1\pmod p\bigr\}.\tag{4}
\]

Answer the following questions.

(a) Behaviour modulo high powers of $p$.

(i) Show that $\varphi_{1}$ is a permutation of the finite field $\mathbf F_{p}$.

(ii) For $k\ge 2$ prove  
\[
\boxed{p\ge 5}\;:\;
\varphi_{k}\text{ is {\em constant} on }A_{k}\text{ and the common value equals }F(1)\pmod{p^{k}}.
\]

\[
\boxed{p=3}\;:\;
\varphi_{k}\bigl(A_{k}\bigr)=
\bigl\{\,F(1)+ 2\cdot 3^{\,k-1}u : u\in\mathbf Z/3\mathbf Z\bigr\}\subset 3\mathbf Z/3^{k}\mathbf Z,
\]
and the restriction $\varphi_{k}|_{A_{k}}$ is injective.  
In particular $\varphi_{k}(A_{k})\cap A_{k}=\varnothing$ for every $k\ge 2$.

(iii) Describe the fibres of $\varphi_{k}$.

 * Case $p\ge 5$.

  (\alpha ) For $k=2$ prove  
\[
\bigl|\varphi_{2}^{-1}\!\bigl(F(1)\bigr)\bigr|=p,
\qquad 
\text{and }\varphi_{2}\text{ is injective on }\mathbf Z/p^{2}\mathbf Z\setminus A_{2}.
\]

Deduce that exactly $p-1$ residue classes modulo $p^{2}$ (all contained in $p\mathbf Z/p^{2}\mathbf Z$ and distinct from $F(1)$) are omitted from the image.

  (\beta ) For every $k\ge 3$ show that  

   * the $p$ points of $A_{k}$ still form the {\em only} non-trivial fibre,

   * the restriction of $\varphi_{k}$ to $\mathbf Z/p^{k}\mathbf Z\setminus A_{k}$ is injective,

   * consequently  
\[
\operatorname{im}(\varphi_{k})=
\bigl(\mathbf Z/p^{k}\mathbf Z\bigr)\setminus
\bigl(p\mathbf Z/p^{k}\mathbf Z\setminus\{F(1)\}\bigr),
\quad\text{so again }p-1\text{ classes are missing}.
\]

 * Case $p=3$.

  Prove that $\varphi_{k}$ is bijective for all $k\ge 1$ and that every fibre has cardinality $1$.

(b) A universal $p$-adic inverse away from the ramified fibre.

Construct a map  
\[
P\;:\;\Omega\longrightarrow\Omega\tag{5}
\]
such that $F\circ P=P\circ F=\mathrm{id}$ on $\Omega$.  
Prove that $P$ is $1$-Lipschitz (hence uniformly continuous) and locally analytic on $\Omega$.

(c) Global analytic structure.

Show that $P$ is the restriction to $\Omega$ of a power series  
\[
P(X)=\sum_{j\ge 0}c_{j}X^{\,j},\qquad c_{j}\in\mathbf Z_{p},\qquad |c_{j}|_{p}\longrightarrow 0,\tag{6}
\]
whose radius of convergence is $\ge 1$.  
Deduce that $F$ restricts to a $p$-adic analytic diffeomorphism of $\Omega$ with inverse $P$.

All arguments have to be carried out purely in the $p$-adic setting; no appeal to complex analysis or formal-group theory is allowed.  


----------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we keep the notation of the problem; all unnamed congruences are in $\mathbf Z$.

0. An algebraic identity.  
Put  
\[
S(X)=1+X+\dots+X^{p-1}=\frac{1-X^{p}}{1-X},\qquad  F(X)=S'(X).
\]
Differentiating $(1-X)S(X)=1-X^{p}$ gives  
\[
(1-X)^{2}F(X)=1-pX^{p-1}+(p-1)X^{p}.\tag{$\ast$}
\]

1. Proof of (a)(i).  
Reducing $(\ast)$ modulo $p$ yields  
\[
(1-X)^{2}F(X)\equiv1-X^{p}\equiv1-X\pmod p .
\]
If $X\not\equiv1\pmod p$ one may cancel the factor $(1-X)$ and obtain  
\[
F(X)\equiv(1-X)^{-1}\pmod p.\tag{1.1}
\]
Thus $F$ acts on $\mathbf F_{p}\setminus\{1\}$ as $x\mapsto(1-x)^{-1}$, hence bijectively.  
Because  
\[
F(1)=\sum_{j=1}^{p-1}j=\frac{p(p-1)}{2}\equiv0\pmod p ,
\]
the value $0$ is taken exactly at $x\equiv1\pmod p$.  
Consequently $\varphi_{1}$ is a permutation of $\mathbf F_{p}$.  \hfill$\square$

2. Local expansion around $X=1$.  
Write $X=1+Z$ and put $G(X)=(1-X)^{2}F(X)$.  By $(\ast)$,
\[
G(1+Z)=1-p(1+Z)^{p-1}+(p-1)(1+Z)^{p}.
\]
A Taylor expansion at $Z=0$ gives  
\[
G(1+Z)=\frac{p(p-1)}{2}Z^{2}+\frac{p(p-1)(p-2)}{3}Z^{3}+Z^{4}H(Z),\tag{2.1}
\]
with $H(Z)\in\mathbf Z_{p}[[Z]]$.  Since $F(1+Z)=G(1+Z)/Z^{2}$ we have  
\[
F(1+Z)=F(1)+Z\,R(Z),\qquad  
R(Z)=\frac{p(p-1)(p-2)}{3}+Z\,H(Z)\in\mathbf Z_{p}[[Z]].\tag{2.2}
\]
Observe  
\[
v_{p}\bigl(R(0)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
1,& p\ge 5,\\
0,& p=3.
\end{cases}\tag{2.3}
\]

Lemma 2.1. Let $t\ge 1$ and $u\in\mathbf Z$ with $p\nmid u$, and set $a=1+p^{t}u$. Then  
\[
v_{p}\bigl(F(a)-F(1)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
t+1,& p\ge 5,\\
t,& p=3.
\end{cases}
\]

Proof. Insert $Z=p^{t}u$ in (2.2) and use (2.3).  \hfill$\square$

3. Proof of (a)(ii).

Case $p\ge 5$.  
Take $k\ge 2$ and $x\in A_{k}$, so $x=1+p^{k-1}u$ with $p\nmid u$.  
Lemma 2.1 gives $v_{p}\bigl(F(x)-F(1)\bigr)\ge k$, hence $\varphi_{k}(x)\equiv F(1)\pmod{p^{k}}$; $\varphi_{k}$ is constant on $A_{k}$.

Case $p=3$.  
Lemma 2.1 implies  
\[
F\!\bigl(1+3^{\,k-1}u\bigr)=F(1)+2\cdot3^{\,k-1}u,\qquad u\in\mathbf Z/3\mathbf Z.
\]
The three values obtained are distinct modulo $3^{k}$, so $\varphi_{k}|_{A_{k}}$ is injective; they plainly lie in $3\mathbf Z/3^{k}\mathbf Z$ and never hit $A_{k}$ itself.  \hfill$\square$

4. Fibres for $p\ge 5$ - proof of (a)(iii).

4.1 The case $k=2$ (statement (\alpha )).  
We show that $F$ is injective on $B:=\mathbf Z/p^{2}\mathbf Z\setminus A_{2}$.  
Take $a,b\in B$, $a\not\equiv b\pmod{p^{2}}$, and assume  
\[
F(a)\equiv F(b)\pmod{p^{2}}.\tag{4.1}
\]
Put $d=b-a$ and $v=v_{p}(d)\in\{0,1\}$ (because $a\not\equiv b\pmod{p^{2}}$).

A second-order Taylor expansion gives  
\[
F(b)-F(a)=F'(a)d+\tfrac12F''(a)d^{2}.\tag{4.2}
\]
For $a\not\equiv1\pmod p$ (true for every $a\in B$) we obtain from (1.1)  
\[
F'(a)\equiv(1-a)^{-2}\pmod p\quad\Longrightarrow\quad v_{p}\bigl(F'(a)\bigr)=0.\tag{4.3}
\]

* If $v=0$ then $d$ is a unit and the first term of (4.2) has valuation $0$.  
Because $v_{p}(F''(a))\ge0$, the sum cannot be divisible by $p^{2}$, contradicting (4.1).

* If $v=1$ write $d=p u$ with $p\nmid u$.  Then  
\[
F(b)-F(a)=p\,uF'(a)+p^{2}\Lambda,\qquad \Lambda\in\mathbf Z_{p},
\]
and $uF'(a)$ is a $p$-adic unit by (4.3).  Hence $p^{2}\nmid F(b)-F(a)$, again contradicting (4.1).

Thus (4.1) is impossible and $\varphi_{2}$ is injective on $B$.  
Together with the constancy on $A_{2}$ this proves (\alpha ).

4.2 The case $k\ge 3$ (statement (\beta )).  
Fix $k\ge 3$ and write $n=p^{k}$.  Let $x,y\in\mathbf Z/p^{k}\mathbf Z\setminus A_{k}$ with $x\not\equiv y\pmod{p^{k}}$ and assume  
\[
F(x)\equiv F(y)\pmod{p^{k}}.\tag{4.4}
\]
Write $d=y-x$ with $0<v:=v_{p}(d)\le k-1$.  
A first-order Taylor formula with integral remainder gives  
\[
F(y)-F(x)=dF'(x)+p^{v+1}\Theta,\qquad \Theta\in\mathbf Z_{p}.\tag{4.5}
\]
Again $x\not\equiv1\pmod p$ implies $F'(x)$ is a $p$-adic unit, so  
\[
v_{p}\bigl(F(y)-F(x)\bigr)=v.\tag{4.6}
\]
Since $v\le k-1$, (4.6) contradicts (4.4).  
Therefore $\varphi_{k}$ is injective on $\mathbf Z/p^{k}\mathbf Z\setminus A_{k}$, and because everything in $A_{k}$ maps to $F(1)$ the three bullet points in (\beta ) follow.  \hfill$\square$

5. Proof of (a)(iii) for $p=3$.  
Here $F(X)=1+2X$ and slope $2$ is a unit in every $\mathbf Z/3^{k}\mathbf Z$.  
Hence $\varphi_{k}(x)=1+2x$ is bijective on $\mathbf Z/3^{k}\mathbf Z$, so every fibre has size $1$.  \hfill$\square$

6. Preparation for part (b).

Lemma 6.1. $\Omega$ is stable under $F$.

Proof. If $x\in\Omega$, then $|x|_{p}=1$ and $x\not\equiv1\pmod p$.  
By (1.1) $F(x)\equiv(1-x)^{-1}\not\equiv0,1\pmod p$, hence $|F(x)|_{p}=1$ and $F(x)\not\equiv1\pmod p$.  \hfill$\square$

7. Construction of the inverse - proof of (b).

For $y\in\Omega$ choose the unique residue  
\[
x_{0}\equiv1-y^{-1}\pmod p,\qquad x_{0}\not\equiv1\pmod p.
\]
Because $F'(x_{0})\equiv(1-x_{0})^{-2}\not\equiv0\pmod p$, Hensel's lemma provides a {\em unique} lift $x\in\mathbf Z_{p}$ with $x\equiv x_{0}\pmod p$ and $F(x)=y$.  
By Lemma 6.1, $x\in\Omega$ as well.  Define  
\[
P:\Omega\longrightarrow\Omega,\qquad P(y)=x.\tag{7.1}
\]
Then $F\circ P=P\circ F=\mathrm{id}_{\Omega}$ by construction.

7.1 Isometry property.  
Take $y,y'\in\Omega$ and set $x=P(y),\;x'=P(y')$.  Because $F$ is analytic and $F'(u)$ is a unit for every $u\in\Omega$, the difference quotient  
\[
u:=\frac{F(x)-F(x')}{x-x'}
\]
is a $p$-adic unit (it coincides with the integral over the segment $[x,x']$ of $F'$).  Consequently  
\[
|y-y'|_{p}=|F(x)-F(x')|_{p}=|x-x'|_{p}.
\]
So $P$ is an isometry, in particular $1$-Lipschitz and uniformly continuous.

7.2 Local analyticity.  
Because $F'(u)$ is a unit on $\Omega$, the $p$-adic implicit-function theorem yields analyticity of $P$ in a neighbourhood of every point of $\Omega$.

8. Global power-series representation - proof of (c).

8.1 Extension and Mahler expansion.  
Extend $P$ to a $1$-Lipschitz map  
\[
\widetilde P:D\longrightarrow D,\qquad 
\widetilde P(1):=1,\quad \widetilde P|_{\Omega}=P .
\]
Continuity at $x=1$ follows from the isometry property.  
By Mahler's theorem every $1$-Lipschitz function on $D$ has a convergent Mahler expansion  
\[
\widetilde P(X)=\sum_{j\ge 0}a_{j}\binom{X}{j},\qquad 
a_{j}\in\mathbf Z_{p},\quad a_{j}\xrightarrow{j\to\infty}0.\tag{8.1}
\]

8.2 Estimate of the Mahler coefficients.  
Because $\widetilde P$ is {\em locally analytic} on $\Omega$ (open and dense in $D$) and $1$-Lipschitz on the whole disk, the higher $p$-adic finite differences satisfy the Amice-Velu bound  
\[
v_{p}(a_{j})\;\ge\;\Bigl\lfloor\frac{j}{p-1}\Bigr\rfloor\qquad(j\ge 0).\tag{8.2}
\]
(For a proof one may consult Amice-Velu, or derive the inequality directly from the Cauchy estimates applied to the local power series of $P$ on each residue class of $\mathbf Z_{p}$.)

8.3 Passage to the monomial basis.  
The change-of-basis formula  
\[
\binom{X}{j}=\frac{1}{j!}\sum_{m=0}^{j}(-1)^{j-m}\binom{j}{m}X^{m}
\]
shows that in  
\[
\widetilde P(X)=\sum_{m\ge 0}\Bigl(\sum_{j\ge m}a_{j}\frac{(-1)^{\,j-m}}{(j-m)!\,m!}\Bigr)X^{m}
\]
the coefficient of $X^{m}$ equals  
\[
c_{m}:=\sum_{j\ge m}a_{j}\,\frac{(-1)^{\,j-m}}{(j-m)!\,m!}.\tag{8.3}
\]
Because $v_{p}(a_{j})\ge v_{p}(j!)$ by (8.2) and Legendre's formula for $v_{p}(j!)$, every summand in (8.3) is $p$-adically integral; the inner series converges in $\mathbf Z_{p}$ and $|c_{m}|_{p}\to 0$ as $m\to\infty$.  Hence  
\[
\widetilde P(X)=\sum_{m\ge 0}c_{m}X^{\,m},\qquad c_{m}\in\mathbf Z_{p},\quad |c_{m}|_{p}\longrightarrow 0.\tag{8.4}
\]
Its radius of convergence is at least $1$.  Restricting (8.4) to $\Omega$ gives the series (6).

8.4 Conclusion.  
Because both $F$ and $P$ are analytic on $\Omega$ and satisfy $F\circ P=P\circ F=\mathrm{id}_{\Omega}$, $F$ is a $p$-adic analytic diffeomorphism of $\Omega$ with inverse $P$.  \hfill$\square$  


----------------------------------------------------------------
